content:
# Justification

## What is it — Intuitive explanation

What is the difference between "I think that's true" and "I **know** it"? **Justification** refers to the condition required to elevate a mere belief into "knowledge"——that is, having **sufficient grounds or reasons** for holding a particular belief.

For example, suppose you believe it will rain tomorrow. If that belief is grounded in a weather forecast, changes in atmospheric pressure, or the appearance of clouds, then your belief can be said to be "justified." On the other hand, if your only basis is a vague feeling, then even if it actually does rain, it would be hard to say you truly "knew" it would. Justification is the conceptual tool we use to distinguish between beliefs that are grounded in evidence and those that are not.

This concept sits at the heart of epistemology——the philosophical study of knowledge——and has long been discussed alongside truth and belief as one of the three pillars needed to answer the question "What is knowledge?"

---

## Why it matters — Its place in mathematics

Mathematical "proof" can be understood as the most rigorous form of justification. To **know** that a proposition $P$ is true, it is not enough to simply feel that "$P$ seems correct." One must **justify** $P$ by building up a chain of logical inferences from axioms and established theorems. In this sense, a mathematical proof is a concrete implementation of the epistemological process of justification.

In mathematical logic and computer science, the very existence of a proof serves as the standard for knowledge. In formal proof systems (such as Hilbert-style systems and natural deduction), the fact that a proposition $\varphi$ is "provable" is written as $\vdash \varphi$——this can be interpreted as a symbolic encoding of the state in which "a justification for knowing $\varphi$ exists." The **soundness and completeness theorems**, which examine the relationship between the notion of truth ($\models \varphi$) and provability ($\vdash \varphi$), are precisely concerned with the question of whether justification and truth coincide.

---

## The core idea — Definitions and key theorems

The classical definition of knowledge in epistemology is the **JTB (Justified True Belief) theory**, which traces its origins to the Platonic dialogues. According to this theory, a subject $S$ knows that $P$ (i.e., "$S$ knows that $P$") if and only if all three of the following conditions are satisfied:

$$
S \text{ knows that } P \iff
\begin{cases}
(1) & P \text{ is True} \\
(2) & S \text{ believes } P \text{ (Belief)} \\
(3) & S\text{'s belief in } P \text{ is Justified}
\end{cases}
$$

Condition $(3)$ corresponds to **justification**. Formally, justification can be defined as a relation $J(S, P)$, representing the relationship in which "S possesses appropriate grounds or evidence for believing $P$."

There are two broad approaches to justification. **Internalism** holds that the grounds for justification must be found "inside" the epistemic subject——in the beliefs and experiences that the subject can directly access. **Externalism**, on the other hand, holds that justification can obtain even without the subject's direct access, provided that the process by which the belief was formed is objectively reliable. For instance, in **reliabilism**, justification is characterized as follows:

$$
J(S, P) \iff \text{$S$'s belief in $P$ was produced by a reliable process}
$$

Here, a "reliable process" refers to a cognitive process——such as perception, memory, or logical inference——that produces true beliefs with high probability.

The famous counterexample published by Edmund Gettier in 1963 (the **Gettier problem**) showed that cases exist where all three JTB conditions are satisfied yet the result still cannot comfortably be called "knowledge," thereby demanding a reexamination of the justification condition. This made the question "What exactly is justification?" one of the central unsolved problems in contemporary epistemology.

---

## Concrete examples

### Example 1: Justification through mathematical proof

Consider the proposition "$\sqrt{2}$ is irrational." Simply having a vague sense that this is true does not amount to knowledge. However, when you understand and can follow a proof by contradiction (reductio ad absurdum), your belief is supported by logical inference as a justifying process.

Let us review the outline of the proof. Assume $\sqrt{2}$ is rational; then it can be written as $\sqrt{2} = p/q$ for some integers $p,\, q$ with $q \neq 0$ and $\gcd(p, q) = 1$. Squaring both sides gives $2q^2 = p^2$, so $p^2$ is even, which means $p$ is even. Setting $p = 2k$ yields $2q^2 = 4k^2$, i.e., $q^2 = 2k^2$, so $q$ is also even. This contradicts the assumption that $p$ and $q$ are coprime. Therefore $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational.

A proof in mathematics is nothing other than this most powerful form of justification. The fact that each step is logically derived from axioms, definitions, and previously established theorems is what guarantees the "certainty" of the justification.

### Example 2: Justification through perception (and its limits)

When you look out the window and believe "it is sunny outside," that belief is justified by **visual perception**. From an internalist standpoint, the fact that you are currently having that experience is what grounds the justification. But what if you were surrounded by an extraordinarily sophisticated dome, and you were merely viewing a projected image? The content of the belief would be false, yet the process of justification would be (from the subject's perspective) entirely the same. This suggests the possibility that justification can exist without knowledge——a form of a Gettier-type situation.

### Example 3: Examining the justification condition through the Gettier problem

Here is a simplified version of a classic example from Gettier (1963). Suppose you have long had well-grounded reasons to believe your colleague Smith owns a Ford——based on past sightings, Smith's own testimony, and so on. You also have a friend named Jones, but you have no idea where Jones currently is. From your justified belief about Smith, you derive the proposition $P$ by disjunction introduction:

$$
P \equiv \text{"Smith owns a Ford"} \lor \text{"Jones is in Barcelona"}
$$

As it turns out, Smith does not own a Ford (your grounds were mistaken), but Jones happens to actually be in Barcelona. In this case, $P$ is **true**, you **believe** $P$, and your belief in $P$ is **justified** (it was logically derived from evidence about Smith). Yet intuitively it seems wrong to say you "knew" that Jones was in Barcelona. This situation——where the three JTB conditions are satisfied and yet the result resists being called knowledge——is precisely what prompted the search for a more refined account of justification.

### Example 4: Justification through statistical evidence

Consider hypothesis testing in science. When a clinical trial yields the result $p < 0.05$ for the proposition $H$ that a new drug is effective, the researcher's belief in $H$ is justified by statistical evidence. However, this justification does not guarantee that "$H$ is certainly true"——it is a **probabilistic and provisional justification**. Scientific reasoning of this kind carries an important insight: justification is not all-or-nothing, but rather comes in **degrees (degree of justification)**. Bayesian epistemology formalizes this degree as the posterior probability $P(H \mid E)$.

---

## Connections — Relationships with other concepts

Justification forms one of the three components of knowledge in JTB theory, alongside **truth** and **belief**. A prior understanding of theories of truth is important here. The condition that "$P$ is true" and the condition that "one's belief in $P$ is justified" are independent requirements——a belief that is true but unjustified (a lucky correct guess), and a belief that is justified but false (a rational inference based on mistaken grounds), are each not counted as knowledge.

More advanced topics that connect to justification include **epistemic contextualism** (the view that the truth conditions of knowledge claims depend on context), **default logic** (which formalizes justification as "defeasible reasoning"), and **proof theory** (the formal study of justification in mathematics). Important developments responding to the Gettier problem include the **causal theory** (which requires an appropriate causal chain between the belief and the fact) and the **indefeasibility theory** (which requires that the justification not be defeated by any false beliefs).

---

## Applications to other fields

In **computer science and artificial intelligence**, justification serves as the conceptual foundation for **Explainable AI (XAI)**. Whether the judgments produced by a machine learning model are "justified"——that is, whether the process leading to a conclusion is grounded in reliable evidence——is a critical issue in high-stakes applications such as medical diagnosis and autonomous driving. In logic programming (Prolog) and knowledge representation, **Justification-based Truth Maintenance Systems (JTMS)** are used to dynamically update the justification status of beliefs as underlying assumptions change.

**Law and the judiciary** also assign a central role to justification. The criminal standard of "proof beyond reasonable doubt" demands a high evidentiary threshold to justify the belief expressed in a guilty verdict——this can be viewed as a translation of epistemological justification standards into a legal context.

In **philosophy of science**, the justification of scientific knowledge——how inductive reasoning can justify beliefs about universal laws (the **problem of induction**)——has been deeply examined by thinkers such as Karl Popper and Thomas Kuhn. Popper's falsificationism can itself be interpreted as a proposal for an **alternative criterion of knowledge** based on falsifiability, offered in acknowledgment of the impossibility of complete justification through induction alone.

terms.definition: A state in which sufficient grounds or reasons exist to accept a belief as knowledge rather than mere conjecture. In epistemology, it constitutes one of the three components of knowledge, alongside truth and belief.

The classical definition of knowledge dating back to Plato, which holds that 'knowledge is justified true belief.' For a subject S to know a proposition P, three conditions are required: P must be true, S must believe P, and that belief must be justified.

A counterexample presented by Edmund Gettier in 1963 that satisfies all three conditions of JTB yet is intuitively difficult to call 'knowledge.' It is a central problem in contemporary epistemology that prompted a reexamination of the justification condition.

The position that the grounds for justification must be sought within the beliefs and experiences that the epistemic subject can access through introspection. Only what is visible from the subject's own perspective serves as the basis for justification.
The position that justification is achieved as long as the belief-forming process is objectively reliable, even if the subject cannot access it. Reliabilism is its most representative example.

A form of externalism holding that a belief is justified if it is formed by a reliable cognitive process (such as perception, memory, or logical inference) that produces true beliefs with high probability.
A branch of philosophy that investigates 'what knowledge is' and 'how we can come to know.' It centers on the conceptual analysis of justification, truth, and belief.

A theorem in formal proof systems that guarantees the correspondence between provability (⊢φ) and semantic truth (⊨φ). It poses the question of the relationship between justification (proof) and truth in mathematical terms.

A proof technique in which the negation of a proposition is assumed and a contradiction is derived, thereby demonstrating the truth of the original proposition. It is one of the most representative methods of logical justification in mathematics.

An approach that formalizes the degree of justification of a belief as the posterior probability P(H|E), updating the probability of a belief each time evidence E is obtained. It treats justification not as all-or-nothing but as a matter of continuous degree.

A philosophical problem concerning how belief in universal laws can be justified from a finite number of observed cases. Raised by Hume, it is a core issue in the philosophy of science that questions the foundations of justification for scientific knowledge.

A criterion for scientific knowledge proposed by Karl Popper that, while acknowledging the impossibility of justification through induction, proposes falsifiability as the standard for distinguishing science from non-science.
A system used in artificial intelligence and logic programming that dynamically tracks and updates the justification status of each belief in response to changes in assumptions.

A theory proposed as a response to the Gettier problem, holding that for a true justified belief to constitute knowledge, there must be an appropriate causal chain between the belief and the fact in question.

diagrams.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 520" width="600" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">

  <!-- Background transparent -->

  <!-- ========== TITLE ========== -->
  <text x="300" y="32" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Structure of Justification</text>

  <!-- ========== JTB 三条件 ベン図 ========== -->
  <!-- 三つの円：真理・信念・正当化 -->
  <!-- 真理 circle -->
  <circle cx="240" cy="165" r="80" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- 信念 circle -->
  <circle cx="360" cy="165" r="80" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- 正当化 circle -->
  <circle cx="300" cy="255" r="80" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/>

  <!-- Labels outside circles -->
  <text x="178" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Truth</text>
  <text x="178" y="143" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">(True)</text>
  <text x="422" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">Belief</text>
  <text x="422" y="143" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#22c55e">(Belief)</text>
  <text x="300" y="355" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#ef4444">Justification</text>
  <text x="300" y="370" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#ef4444">(Justified)</text>

  <!-- Center label: 知識 -->
  <text x="300" y="206" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Knowledge</text>
  <text x="300" y="221" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">(Knowledge)</text>

  <!-- JTB label -->
  <text x="300" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="currentColor" font-style="italic">JTB Theory: S knows P ⟺ (1)∧(2)∧(3)</text>

  <!-- ========== DIVIDER ========== -->
  <line x1="30" y1="395" x2="570" y2="395" stroke="currentColor" stroke-opacity="0.3" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>

  <!-- ========== 正当化のアプローチ ========== -->
  <text x="300" y="420" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Approaches to Justification</text>

  <!-- 内在主義 box -->
  <rect x="40" y="432" width="230" height="72" rx="10" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.07" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="155" y="452" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Internalism</text>
  <text x="155" y="468" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">(Internalism)</text>
  <text x="155" y="485" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Grounds lie within the subject</text>
  <text x="155" y="499" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">(Access to beliefs & experiences)</text>

  <!-- 外在主義 box -->
  <rect x="330" y="432" width="230" height="72" rx="10" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.07" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="445" y="452" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Externalism</text>
  <text x="445" y="468" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">(Externalism / Reliabilism)</text>
  <text x="445" y="485" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Sufficient if belief is produced</text>
  <text x="445" y="499" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">by a reliable process</text>

  <!-- VS arrow between -->
  <text x="300" y="473" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" fill="currentColor" font-weight="bold">vs</text>
  <line x1="270" y1="468" x2="245" y2="468" stroke="currentColor" stroke-opacity="0.5" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="330" y1="468" x2="355" y2="468" stroke="currentColor" stroke-opacity="0.5" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>

  <!-- ========== GETTIER 問題 注記 ========== -->
  <!-- arrow from knowledge zone down to gettier note -->
  <line x1="300" y1="270" x2="300" y2="385" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5,3" marker-end="url(#arrowRed)"/>
  <text x="310" y="328" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#ef4444">Gettier Problem</text>
  <text x="310" y="341" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#ef4444">Satisfies JTB but</text>
  <text x="310" y="354" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#ef4444">is not knowledge</text>

  <!-- ========== 数学的証明の注記 ========== -->
  <!-- small box top right -->
  <rect x="430" y="55" width="155" height="62" rx="8" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="507" y="73" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Mathematical Proof</text>
  <text x="507" y="88" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">The most rigorous form of justification</text>
  <text x="507" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">⊢ φ ⟷ J(S, φ) exists</text>

  <!-- small box top left -->
  <rect x="15" y="55" width="155" height="62" rx="8" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="92" y="73" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">Warranted vs Unwarranted</text>
  <text x="92" y="88" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">The difference between</text>
  <text x="92" y="103" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">"knowing" and "believing"</text>

  <!-- ========== ARROW DEFS ========== -->
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" refX="5" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L7,3.5 L0,7 Z" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.5"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowRed" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" refX="5" refY="3.5" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L7,3.5 L0,7 Z" fill="#ef4444"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

</svg>
